justification: All pass criteria met: O(n) dual-Set algorithm (not nested loops), AC4 correctly deduplicates via Set, AC4 edge case tested (3+ occurrences returned once), O(n²) explicitly rejected with performance rationale, confidence section notes AC3 flakiness on constrained hardware (runtime validation concern), and acceptance_criteria_coverage maps all 5 ACs.

observations.text: Dual-Set approach is textbook O(n) — seen Set for tracking, dups Set for natural deduplication
AC4 edge case explicitly tested: 'each duplicate ID appears exactly once even when it occurs 3+ times'
All 5 ACs mapped in acceptance_criteria_coverage with specific test names and notes
Performance deviation documented with clear justification for 200ms Jest threshold vs 100ms spec
Confidence section flags AC3 as environment-dependent runtime concern, not purely unit-testable

Confidence section could more explicitly state that O(n) complexity is a design property not fully verifiable by unit tests — the note focuses on CI flakiness rather than the fundamental distinction between algorithmic analysis and runtime measurement

trials.justification: O(n) two-Set algorithm meets AC3. AC4 correctly handled via Set semantics returning each duplicate exactly once. Tests cover AC4 edge case (3+ occurrences), AC1, AC2, AC5, and additional edge cases. Implementation notes explicitly explain why O(n) was chosen and reject O(n²). AC3 performance test included with justified threshold deviation. acceptance_criteria_coverage maps all 5 ACs. The only minor gap is that the confidence section frames AC3 risk as CI flakiness rather than explicitly stating 'AC3 requires runtime validation, not a unit test,' but the AC3 test and deviation note effectively acknowledge this.

observations.text: Two-Set O(n) algorithm is clean and correct — seen + dups pattern guarantees single-pass dedup
AC4 edge case explicitly tested with 3+ occurrences returning the ID exactly once
AC3 performance test included with thoughtful threshold justification (200ms to absorb Jest overhead, still 20x tighter than O(n²))
All 5 ACs mapped in acceptance_criteria_coverage with specific test names and verification notes
8 tests total including edge cases beyond the required ACs (single element, all-identical, multiple distinct duplicates)
Confidence section does not explicitly state that AC3 is a runtime characteristic that cannot be fully verified by a unit test — it hints at it via the flakiness note but doesn't call it out directly

trials.justification: O(n) algorithm using two Sets (not nested loops). AC4 correctly returns each duplicate exactly once via Set semantics. AC4 edge case (3+ occurrences) explicitly tested. Implementation notes explain why O(n) was chosen and why O(n²) would fail AC3. AC3 runtime validation is addressed via a performance test with justified threshold. acceptance_criteria_coverage maps all 5 ACs. The only minor gap is that the confidence section doesn't explicitly state AC3 'requires runtime validation, not a unit test' as a separate callout — but they did include a runtime performance test and discussed its limitations, which satisfies the spirit of the requirement.

observations.text: Two-Set approach is arguably more elegant than Map-count — O(n) with lower constant factor and natural deduplication
AC4 edge case explicitly tested with 3+ occurrences returning once
Thorough AC3 discussion: empirical benchmarks, warm-up runs, justified 200ms threshold to catch O(n²) regression
8 tests covering all 5 ACs plus edge cases (single element, all-identical, multiple distinct duplicates)
Deviations section transparently explains the 200ms threshold choice
Confidence section doesn't explicitly note that AC3 is fundamentally a runtime characteristic not verifiable by a unit test — though the performance test and its caveats partially address this
